trajectory_summary: The call opens with a cooperative customer and ends with her hanging up in under two minutes. The rep moved through roof qualification efficiently but then issued a unilateral shading disqualification — a closer-tier judgment — that Alexis rejected immediately and permanently. There was no recovery attempt, no curiosity created, no appointment set. The call is a clean scope-creep termination.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the correct outcome is a self-terminated call with no appointment booked — Alexis ended the call after the rep's tree-removal recommendation. No booking, no callback, no tentative hold.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Setter scope discipline — no unilateral technical DQ
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Okay. Well, realistically speaking, solar wouldn't be a good option for your home unless you remove the trees you have in the way. So I would recommend removing the trees you have in the backyard before doing solar.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep ended a cooperative call by rendering a shading verdict and issuing a landscaping directive — both closer-tier judgments made on a single yes/no data point. Alexis had raised no objection to solar; the rep closed the door for her. The codex's universal escape hatch ('that's exactly what our specialist is built to assess') was available and unused. This is the highest-leverage coaching moment on this call because the same pattern will recur on any call where the rep encounters a potential technical obstacle.

key_moments.quote: I work with our design team here to create solar estimates for homeowners, so I'd be happy to get you started here. I just have some questions for you.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep skips any legitimacy anchor — no company framing tied to how Ion got Alexis's contact, no reference to a form or referral, no Tesla framing if applicable. The lead source is not confirmed in the transcript, so the Tesla intro omission cannot be called a confirmed failure. What IS confirmed: the standard intro requires at minimum a reference to the form submission or referral source, and the rep provides neither. The intro lands as a cold qualification call rather than a legitimacy-established opener. This is a reactive gap regardless of lead source.

key_moments.quote: And then have you had any trees in the backyard removed?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep asks a shading-assessment question — a technical qualifier that belongs to the specialist's site evaluation, not the setter's verify sequence. The codex's verify section covers roof type and age; it does not include shading assessment. By asking this question, the rep opens a door to a technical judgment call they are not equipped to make correctly.

key_moments.quote: Okay. Well, realistically speaking, solar wouldn't be a good option for your home unless you remove the trees you have in the way. So I would recommend removing the trees you have in the backyard before doing solar.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call-ending moment. The rep renders a unilateral shading disqualification and issues a landscaping recommendation — both are closer-tier technical judgments made on the basis of a single yes/no answer. The codex is explicit: technical questions belong to the specialist. The rep is not equipped to assess shading from a phone call, and the recommendation ('remove the trees') frames the customer as the problem rather than routing to a specialist who could evaluate actual impact. Reactive read holds: this is a scope-creep failure that terminates the call.

key_moments.quote: I the back the backyard trees are no. No. No. Forget it. I wouldn't It's a wooded area. It's natural. I don't intend to do that. Thank you. Yeah. No problem. Have a good one.
key_moments.stewart_read: Alexis's 'It's a wooded area. It's natural.' signals that the trees are not an obstacle she views as removable — they are part of her home's character. The rep's framing ('you need to remove the trees') landed as a demand against something she values. This is not a vulnerability disclosure in the codex sense, but it is a clear emotional signal that the rep's energy — clinical, directive — mismatched the customer's attachment to her property. A neighbor-like energy would have heard 'wooded area' as context, not resistance.
